I. INTRODUCTION
[1] The plaintiff is a naturopathic physician. That profession is governed by the defendant, the College of Naturopathic Physicians of British Columbia (the "College"), pursuant to the Health Professions Act, RSBC 1996, c 183 (the "HP Act").
[2] The plaintiff has not received any vaccinations against COVID-19 ("Covid"). She believes that natural immunity flowing from Covid infection affords significant protection from infection and further transmission of Covid. She pleads that she fears and anticipates that the defendant, Provincial Health Officer ("PH Officer"[1]), will issue an order requiring all naturopathic doctors to be vaccinated, and that this order will not contain appropriate exemptions to ensure its compliance with the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms, Part I of the Constitution Act, 1982, being Schedule B to the Canada Act 1982 (UK), 1982, c 11.
[3] Throughout the height of the pandemic and to date, the PH Officer has never issued an order that would require the plaintiff, who is not employed in the public health care system, to be vaccinated in order to provide her services. Nor has the defendant College ever imposed such a requirement. As expressly stated in the PH Officer Information Bulletin of September 15, 2022:
The scope of these orders has not changed. They continue to apply to people who work for health authorities, agencies funded by health authorities and long-term care and assisted-living facilities.
They do not, however, apply to regulated health professionals who do not work in these settings, such as dentists, naturopathic doctors and opticians.[2]
[emphasis added]
[4] In her claim, the plaintiff seeks damages, injunctions, and various declarations against the defendants the College and His Majesty the King in Right of the Province of British Columbia (in Right of the PH Officer and Minister of Health) (the latter one or two or three entities - it is not clear -- the "provincial defendants") for various actual and anticipated enactments and actions pursuant to the Emergency Program Act, RSBC 1996, c 111 and the Public Health Act, SBC 2008, c 28 (the "PH Act"). She claims that the defendants' decisions and actions lack statutory foundation and have breached her ss. 2(b) (expression), and 7 (life, liberty, and security of the person) Charter rights. The plaintiff brings her claim in the form of a private action by way of a notice of civil claim, in contrast to the vast majority of litigation challenges to Covid measures, which have been brought as judicial reviews by way of petitions, pursuant to the Judicial Review Procedure Act, RSBC 1996, c 241.[3]
[5] The defendants apply to strike the plaintiff's claim under Rule 9-5(1) of the Supreme Court Civil Rules, BC Reg 168/2009, and dismiss the action. In response, the plaintiff has brought an application to amend her notice of civil claim to add 24 new paragraphs and subparagraphs, including a new tort claim, in intimidation.
[6] Across Canada, litigants have filed many claims based on governmental and regulatory enactments in response to the Covid pandemic. As Courts decide those cases, the jurisprudential landscape, on which this Court must base its decision, changes. These new cases include, most recently, Hoogerbrug v British Columbia, 2024 BCSC 794, which examines many of the same issues in the present case. Further, the administrative landscape has changed, with new health orders and directives, in light of the changing epidemiological conditions. The Court has invited, and the parties have provided, periodic submissions reflecting these evolving developments. The Court has also informed the parties in the present proceeding that it will consider and decide this application in tandem with the proposed class proceeding certification proceedings in Canadian Society for the Advancement of Science in Public Policy v. His Majesty the King in Right of the Province of British Columbia and Dr. Bonnie Henry in her Capacity as Provincial Health Officer for the Province of British Columbia (Vancouver Action No. S210831), which raises several issues overlapping with the present case, with the same defendant represented by the same counsel as the present proceeding. These are those reasons.
[7] For the reasons set out below, the Court strikes the existing notice of civil claim ("NCC"), without leave to amend, and dismisses the plaintiff's application to amend. As no defendant has ever made a public health order or directive compelling the vaccination of naturopath physicians such as the plaintiff, it is premature. Further, the claim is a thinly-veiled collateral attack on administrative decisions and actions carried out in the course of legislatively-bestowed duties, that seeks in its essence to challenge, overturn, and bar the effects of those administrative decisions. As a challenge to the exercise of regulatory authority, the proceeding must be brought as a judicial review of those decisions and actions, in the form of a petition. Further, the claim suffers from myriad other defects, including a failure to plead material facts, pleading impermissible evidence and argument, misnomer of proper defendants, advancement of Charter damages where it is not appropriate and just to do so, and advancement of claims not constituting proper causes of action.
[8] In contrast to many applications to strike, where the plaintiff is given leave to provide a hypothetical amended claim that will address the pleading deficiencies, in the present application the plaintiff has already provided her proposed amended notice of civil claim (the "PANCC") to consider. This proposed amended claim suffers from the same fatal flaws as the existing pleading, and cannot be fixed through further amendment. The claim is struck out and dismissed.
II. THE CLAIM
A. The existing notice of civil claim (NCC)
[9] In her NCC, the plaintiff pleads that she is a naturopathic physician and registrant of the defendant College. She claims that she recovered from her Covid infection in January 2022. She states that she does not wish to receive any Covid vaccinations.
[10] Under the heading of "Restriction of Free Expression with respect to Natural Immunity from Coronavirus", the plaintiff pleads that she believes that people tend to develop natural immunity from Covid after a Covid infection, as well as other beliefs about that disease and its treatment (which she defines as the "Natural Immunity Beliefs"). She also pleads certain beliefs downplaying the efficacy of vaccines, and criticising the PH Officer's response to the Covid pandemic (the "Critical Beliefs").
[11] The plaintiff alleges that the College, through various notices and practice directives, has restricted her right of free expression in prohibiting her from expressing her "Natural Immunity Beliefs" and "Critical Beliefs". She claims that various communications by the College to its registrants to the effect that "[a]ny statements by naturopathic doctors about the prevention and/or treatment of COVID-19, beyond the information made available by the public health authorities, are inappropriate, potentially harmful, and likely to violate the [College]'s Advertising Policy and/or Immunization Standard",[4] constitute "Threats" and "Expressive Restrictions". She alleges that the PH Officer "encouraged, directed, pressured and ordered" the College to impose the "Expressive Restrictions" and issue the "Threats". She claims that the "Expressive Restrictions" violated her Charter s. 2(b) rights.
[12] Under the heading of "Forced Medical Treatment", the plaintiff pleads that at various times between October 2021 and March 2022, the PH Officer made statements indicating that she may require health professionals, including those regulated by the College, to be vaccinated at a future date. She pleads and relies on a March 7, 2022 order issued by the PH Officer under the PH Act, in which the PH Officer stated her belief that an unvaccinated registrant who provides services to persons puts persons at risk of infection with Covid, and constitutes a "health hazard" under the PH Act.
[13] The plaintiff pleads that she anticipates that the PH Officer, acting under the authority of the PH Act, will, in the future, require her to be vaccinated against Covid in order to continue practising naturopathic medicine. She further pleads that she anticipates that any future public health order requiring naturopathic physicians to be vaccinated will not provide an exemption from vaccination in various circumstances and will not provide for reasonable alternatives to vaccination.
[14] She claims that the anticipated requirement that she receive a vaccination in order to practice as a naturopathic physician "infringes her medical autonomy, which is an aspect of her liberty and security of the person protected by s.7 of the Charter".
[15] The plaintiff pleads that the combination of the alleged "Expressive Restrictions" and the anticipated requirement of vaccination to practice constitute a "Toxic Synergy of Infringements".
[16] The plaintiff asks the Court to issue declarations that:
a) the College's "Expressive Restrictions" constitute a breach of s. 2(b) of the Charter;
b) the plaintiff is not herself a "public health hazard", as defined by the PH Act;
c) the "imposition of forced treatment" by the PH Officer constitutes an infringement of s. 7 of the Charter; and
d) the PH Officer's emergency powers under the PH Act are of no force and effect to the extent they are inconsistent with s. 7 of the Charter.
[17] The plaintiff also seeks an order declaring the emergency powers under the PH Act to be of no force and effect to the extent of their inconsistency with the Charter.
[18] The plaintiff also seeks an order pursuant to s. 24(1) of the Charter, asking the Court to "read in" specific limitations to ss. 51, 52, and 53 of the PH Act, such as to restrict the powers of the PH Officer to issue directions to address an "emergency" or a "regional event".
[19] The plaintiff also seeks interim, interlocutory, and permanent injunctions preventing the defendants from imposing the "Expressive Restrictions" and requiring the anticipated vaccinations.
[20] Finally, the plaintiff also seeks damages pursuant to s. 24(1) of the Charter, special damages, and costs, including special costs and elevated costs, on a full indemnity basis.
B. The proposed amended notice of civil claim (PANCC)
[21] After receiving the defendants' application materials to strike her claim, the plaintiff filed her application for leave to file an amended notice of civil claim. As the plaintiff had already unilaterally filed her own notice of trial (over the defendants' objections), she requires either the defendants' consent (which has not been granted), or an order of the Court to amend her claim: Rule 6-1(1).
[22] The plaintiff's proposed amendments to the factual basis of her claim (Part 1) include that:
a) she reasonably believes that her Charter rights will be breached (para. 39);
b) the provincial defendants have acted in bad faith because, to their knowledge, the available vaccines are not completely effective against preventing transmission of Covid, and the provincial defendants did not adequately disclose this to the public (para. 40);
c) the PH Officer is acting in bad faith because she declared a state of emergency under the PH Act in order to accomplish public health objectives that could have been accomplished through other means (para. 41);
d) the provincial defendants directed and influenced the College to make an administrative decision respecting the regulation of naturopathic physicians (paras. 42-44);
e) the defendants (collectively) continue to threaten the plaintiff with unspecified fines, imprisonment, and regulatory consequences (para. 45);
f) public statements and administrative decisions made by the PH Officer were intended to cause the plaintiff significant stress and anxiety (paras. 46-50); and
g) the plaintiff sustained business losses as a result of an administrative decision made by the College and the "anticipated breach" of her right to liberty and security of the person (paras. 51-52).
[23] The plaintiff's proposed amendments to the relief sought (Part 2) add a further provision to read into the PH Act pursuant to s. 24(1) of the Charter:
vi. Specifying that a person is not included within the meaning of a "health hazard" under the Public Health Act, by inserting the words "does not include a person" into the definition between the words "hazard" and "means".[5]
[emphasis in original]
[24] The plaintiff's proposed amendments to the legal basis of her claim (Part 3) seek the following relief:
a) a declaration that the power in the PH Act to declare that a person is a "health hazard" infringes s. 7 of the Charter and a remedy under s. 24(1) (para. 17);
b) a declaration of invalidity and damages in relation to the PH Officer's finding that unvaccinated health professionals providing services constitute a "health hazard" (para. 26); and
c) "monetary compensation" (presumably damages) for state-induced psychological stress and intimidation in relation to the pleadings outlined above (para. 27).
III. DECISION AND DISCUSSION
A. Introduction
[25] The defendants seek an order striking the plaintiff's claim and dismissing the plaintiff's application to amend, under subrules 9-5(1)(b) and (d), on the basis that the NCC and proposed amendments are unnecessary, frivolous, vexatious, and an abuse of process, as well as under subrule 9-5(1)(a), on the basis that the NCC and proposed amendments do not disclose a reasonable claim.
[26] Rule 9-5(1) reads:
Scandalous, frivolous or vexatious matters
(1) At any stage of a proceeding, the court may order to be struck out or amended the whole or any part of a pleading, petition or other document on the ground that
(a) it discloses no reasonable claim or defence, as the case may be,
(b) it is unnecessary, scandalous, frivolous or vexatious,
(c) it may prejudice, embarrass or delay the fair trial or hearing of the proceeding, or
(d) it is otherwise an abuse of the process of the court,
and the court may pronounce judgment or order the proceeding to be stayed or dismissed and may order the costs of the application to be paid as special costs.
[27] In R. v. Imperial Tobacco Canada Ltd., 2011 SCC 42, the Supreme Court of Canada explained the important housekeeping function provided by civil procedure strike provisions:
[20] This promotes two goods -- efficiency in the conduct of the litigation and correct results. Striking out claims that have no reasonable prospect of success promotes litigation efficiency, reducing time and cost. The litigants can focus on serious claims, without devoting days and sometimes weeks of evidence and argument to claims that are in any event hopeless. The same applies to judges and juries, whose attention is focused where it should be -- on claims that have a reasonable chance of success. The efficiency gained by weeding out unmeritorious claims in turn contributes to better justice. The more the evidence and arguments are trained on the real issues, the more likely it is that the trial process will successfully come to grips with the parties' respective positions on those issues and the merits of the case.
B. Subrules (b) and (d): abuse of process and collateral attack
1. The claim constitutes a collateral attack and abuse of process
[28] Under subrules 9-5(1)(b) and (d), the defendants argue that the claim is, in essence, an impermissible collateral attack on the decisions and actions of the provincial defendants and the College, which are properly the subject of a judicial review brought by way of petition, not by way of action, as is in the present proceeding: see Canada (Attorney General) v. TeleZone Inc., 2010 SCC 62 at paras. 60-62 [TeleZone]. They also argue that it should be struck under the flexible and broad doctrine of abuse of process, as expressly codified in subrule 9-5(1)(d). Were the proceeding properly brought as a judicial review, the Court would be obliged to approach those governmental decisions and actions under the deferential approach mandated by Loyola High School v. Quebec (Attorney General), 2015 SCC 12 and Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v. Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, asking whether the decisions and actions were reasonable in light of the relevant statutory mandate, and evaluated solely on the record underlying those decisions. To permit this action for damages and declarations to proceed would bypass the decisions and actions of the PH Officer on highly technical scientific issues with which she has been entrusted by the democratically-elected legislature. It would assess the defendants' decisions and actions on a standard of correctness, rather than reasonableness. It would eschew the deference to which she and other designated administrative decision makers, particularly those in a highly technical field of expertise, as here, are entitled. It would place the reviewing court in the untenable position of assessing matters afresh, on an expanded record, as an inappropriate and ill-equipped "armchair epidemiologist": Beaudoin v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2022 BCCA 427 at para. 156, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 40622 (10 August 2023).
[29] In response, the plaintiff argues that she raises a comprehensive claim dealing centrally with the constitutional validity of emergency powers under the PH Act. She argues that it is appropriate to adjudicate the constitutional validity of the PH Act through an action. She notes that she is not only seeking declarations concerning the decisions and actions of the defendants, and injunctions against enforcement of those administrative decisions, but is also seeking Charter damages payable by all of the defendants pursuant to s. 24(1) to compensate her, to vindicate her rights, and to deter further infringements. Were this matter be pursued as a judicial review, she would not be able to obtain orders for that financial compensation. She also claims that the court would be unable to issue constitutional remedies on judicial review.
[30] The Court agrees with the defendants' arguments, on all grounds.
[31] A collateral attack is "an attack made in proceedings other than those whose specific object is the reversal, variation, or nullification of the order or judgment": TeleZone at para. 60, citing Wilson v. The Queen, [1983] 2 SCR 594 at 599. The rule against collateral attack seeks to protect the proper administration of justice through a party's improper avoidance of a decision rendered against it. As explained in TeleZone at para. 61, citing the following passage from Garland v. Consumers' Gas Co., 2004 SCC 25 at para. 72:
The fundamental policy behind the rule against collateral attack is to "maintain the rule of law and to preserve the repute of the administration of justice" (R. v. Litchfield, [1993] 4 S.C.R. 333, at p. 349). The idea is that if a party could avoid the consequences of an order issued against it by going to another forum, this would undermine the integrity of the justice system. Consequently, the doctrine is intended to prevent a party from circumventing the effect of a decision rendered against it.
[emphasis in original]
[32] Abuse of process is a flexible doctrine by which a court prevents a claim from proceeding where it would violate principles of judicial economy, consistency, finality, and the integrity of the administration of justice: Willow v. Chong, 2013 BCSC 1083 at para. 21. British Columbia (Workers' Compensation Board) v. Figliola, 2011 SCC 52 [Figliola] sets out the purposes and manifestations of the abuse of process doctrine:
[34] At their heart, the foregoing doctrines exist to prevent unfairness by preventing "abuse of the decision-making process" (Danyluk, at para. 20; see also Garland, at para. 72, and Toronto (City), at para. 37). Their common underlying principles can be summarized as follows:
- It is in the interests of the public and the parties that the finality of a decision can be relied on (Danyluk, at para. 18; Boucher, at para. 35).
- Respect for the finality of a judicial or administrative decision increases fairness and the integrity of the courts, administrative tribunals and the administration of justice; on the other hand, relitigation of issues that have been previously decided in an appropriate forum may undermine confidence in this fairness and integrity by creating inconsistent results and unnecessarily duplicative proceedings (Toronto (City), at paras. 38 and 51).
- The method of challenging the validity or correctness of a judicial or administrative decision should be through the appeal or judicial review mechanisms that are intended by the legislature (Boucher, at para. 35; Danyluk, at para. 74).
- Parties should not circumvent the appropriate review mechanism by using other forums to challenge a judicial or administrative decision (TeleZone, at para. 61; Boucher, at para. 35; Garland, at para. 72).
- Avoiding unnecessary relitigation avoids an unnecessary expenditure of resources (Toronto (City), at paras. 37 and 51).
[emphasis added]
[33] Initiating a civil action instead of seeking judicial review may constitute a collateral attack on a statutory decision, and therefore an abuse of process: Greengen Holdings Ltd. v. British Columbia (Ministry of Forests, Lands and Natural Resource Operations), 2018 BCCA 214 at para. 31, citing Stewart v. Clark, 2013 BCCA 359 at para. 39.
[34] Both doctrines of collateral attack and abuse of the court's process engage "the inherent power of the court to prevent the misuse of its procedure in a way that would be manifestly unfair to a party to the litigation before it or would in some other way bring the administration of justice into disrepute": Canam Enterprises Inc. v. Coles (2000), 51 OR (3d) 481 (CA) at para. 55 (per Goudge JA), rev'd 2002 SCC 63 (aff'g dissent of Goudge JA). The purpose of the doctrines is "to prevent unfairness by preventing 'abuse of the decision-making process'": Figliola at para. 34; Quinn v. British Columbia, 2018 BCCA 320 at para. 77, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 38375 (21 March 2019).
[35] TeleZone and its subsequent jurisprudence establish that an individual who disagrees with an administrative decision has two options:
(a) seek judicial review to have the decision overturned, quashed, or otherwise invalidated; or
(b) abide by the decision and let it stand, and file an action raising a viable private law cause of action, if one exists in the circumstances.
[36] As stated in TeleZone:
[19] If a claimant seeks to set aside the order of a federal decision maker, it will have to proceed by judicial review, as the Grenier court held. However, if the claimant is content to let the order stand and instead seeks compensation for alleged losses (as here), there is no principled reason why it should be forced to detour to the Federal Court for the extra step of a judicial review application (itself sometimes a costly undertaking) when that is not the relief it seeks. Access to justice requires that the claimant be permitted to pursue its chosen remedy directly and, to the greatest extent possible, without procedural detours.
[emphasis added]
[37] In a series of decisions considering TeleZone, our Court of Appeal has examined whether each specific civil action before it constituted an abuse of process as a collateral attack on a governmental decision that should properly be challenged through judicial review, either in this Court or another forum: Stewart; Greengen; Northern Cross (Yukon) Ltd. v. Yukon (Energy, Mines and Resources), 2021 YKCA 6, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 40053 (4 August 2022) [Northern Cross] (three members of our Court of Appeal sitting as the Yukon Court of Appeal); Myers v. Canada (Attorney General), 2022 BCCA 160 at para. 34; and Canada (Attorney General) v. Scow, 2022 BCCA 275 [Scow].
[38] Factually, Stewart, Northern Cross, and Greengen are more germane to the present scenario, in that the Court considered whether the pursuit of a private claim instead of a judicial review in the same Court constituted a collateral attack on an administrative or governmental decision. The remaining cases examine whether the claimants were obliged to bring their claims as judicial reviews in other courts and fora, such as the Tax Court or Federal Court: Myers and Scow, as well as TeleZone, as decided by the Supreme Court of Canada. The principles remain the same in either context.
[39] The present plaintiff emphasised Myers and Greengen: those decisions declined to strike the private law damages claims, which did not seek to overturn or avoid the effects of administrative decisions and actions, but rather cited their alleged unlawfulness as a necessary basis for private tort claims.
[40] In Myers, the plaintiffs filed a notice of civil claim against the federal government, claiming misfeasance in public office, negligence, abuse of process, and violation of their Charter rights in relation to certain tax reassessments and other CRA conduct. The Court found that the plaintiffs were not seeking to avoid the legal effect of the tax assessments, but were relying on the unlawfulness of the assessments as a material fact supporting their civil tort claims: see para. 43.
[41] In Greengen, similarly, the plaintiff did not seek to avoid the consequences of the denial by the respondent Ministry of Forests of a water licence for its hydropower project, or enjoin that decision. Rather, the plaintiff cited the allegedly unlawful governmental actions as facts material and necessary for its private claim against the government for misfeasance in public office: paras. 58-62.
[42] Greengen explains the two ways in which a civil action may constitute an impermissible collateral attack:
[32] However, a civil action will not be a collateral attack only because it alleges that a government decision is unlawful. TeleZone confirms that a civil action may constitute a collateral attack in two ways.
[33] The first (and most obvious) way is where the civil action seeks to invalidate the decision or avoid its consequences. A plaintiff in a civil action cannot claim relief that is only available on judicial review, such as an order setting aside the decision. To seek such a remedy in a private law action effectively circumvents the deference that is usually given to statutory decision makers. It is important to note, however, that the doctrine applies only where the action attacks the legal force of the decision; it does not apply where the action attacks the factual basis for the decision: Toronto (City) at para. 34.
[34] The second is where the plaintiff fails to plead a valid cause of action for damages. The superior courts' inherent jurisdiction includes a residual discretion to stay a claim for damages where in its "essential character it is a claim for judicial review with only a thin pretence to a private wrong": TeleZone at para. 78.
[emphasis added]
[43] Myers emphasises that plaintiffs will only be permitted to pursue an action rather than seek relief via judicial review if they are content to accept the legal force of the governmental decision:
[32] In my respectful view, the judge misread TeleZone because she failed to distinguish between a challenge to the lawfulness of a government decision, and a challenge to its legal force and effect. The distinction is a critical one, but is easily missed because of imprecise terminology in the jurisprudence. Judges use "validity of a decision" to refer to both the lawfulness of decisions and the legal force of decisions--two distinct concepts. An example may assist. If a judge makes an order which, on appeal, is subsequently determined to be based on a legal error--in plain speech, to be "wrong"--the order nonetheless has legal force and effect from the moment it is made and must be obeyed unless and until it is challenged in the correct forum and set aside. The order in this example is unlawful--wrong--from the moment it is made, but it nonetheless is valid, having legal force and effect, until it is set aside on appeal.
[33] When TeleZone speaks of a party being content to "let the decision stand", it refers to a party who accepts the legal force of the decision in the sense that they accept that they are bound to abide by the decision and do not seek in the provincial superior court to overturn the decision or nullify its effects. But TeleZone does not say that the party may not, as part of a properly framed civil cause of action, seek to prove the unlawfulness of that federal decision as a material fact supporting their claim for damages.
[emphasis in original]
[44] The court must consider the essential nature of the claim. As stated in Scow:
[75] The essential nature of the claim must be based on a realistic appreciation of the practical result sought by the claimant. A judge must look beyond the words used in the pleading, the facts alleged, and the remedy sought to be satisfied that the claim is not an "artfully pleaded" but thinly disguised attempt to pursue a claim in an inappropriate forum
[emphasis in original]
[45] A review of both the NCC and PANCC reveal that the essence of the plaintiff's claim is a challenge to and an attempt to avoid the legal force and effect of the decisions and actions of the defendants in the exercise of their statutorily-bestowed powers and responsibilities. The present and proposed pleadings also fail to plead a valid cause of action. The claim falls afoul of both forms of collateral attack set out in Greengen.
[46] This can be illustrated by comparing the relief sought in the present pleadings with that sought in Greengen and Myers: the claims that survived strike applications. In Myers and Greengen, the Part 2 (Relief Sought) lists are brief: the plaintiffs merely sought general and special damages (the Myers plaintiffs also sought punitive and aggravated damages). No declarations were sought challenging the validity, or seeking to overturn, enjoin, avoid, or amend governmental decisions, orders, enactments, or actions, in stark contrast to the expansive remedies demanded here by the plaintiff, Ms Weisenburger:
a) A declaration that the imposition of the Expressive Restrictions by the College constitute a breach of s.2(b) of the Charter;
b) A declaration that the plaintiff does not herself constitute a "public health hazard" as defined by the Public Health Act;
c) A declaration that imposition of forced treatment by the PHO and the College constitute an infringement of the right to liberty and security of person and infringement of the principle of overbreadth, disproportionality, arbitrariness and the principle of compliance with the rule of law, pursuant to s.7 of the Charter;
d) An order declaring the emergency powers under the Public Health Act to be of no force and effect to the extent of their inconsistency with the Charter;
e) An order "reading in" the following limits to the emergency powers under the Public Health Act pursuant to s.24(1) of the Charter:
i. Restricting the definition of "emergency" and/or "regional event" under ss.51, 52 and 53 of the Public Health Act to events that cannot be addressed by means of non-emergency powers;
ii. Restricting the exercise of powers under s.52(2) of the Public Health Act to regional events that are "unusual or unexpected", in lieu of making "unusual or unexpected" one of four criteria, the presence of two of which will trigger the existence of powers, and substituting the numeral "1" for the number "2" under s.52(2) of the Public Health Act;
iii. Restricting the discretion of the PHO to decisions, the effects of which are proportionate to the "emergency" or "regional event";
iv. Restricting the discretion of the PHO under s.53(2) of the Public Health Act to decisions that do not contravene the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms and/or do not contravene s.6 of the Health Care (Consent) and Care Facility (Admission) Act, RSBC 1996, c.181; and/or
v. Requiring the PHO to seek prior judicial authorization of restrictions of Charter and non-Charter rights under s.53 of the Public Health Act, in the absence of exigent circumstances; and
vi. Specifying that a person is not included within the meaning of a "health hazard" under the Public Health Act, by inserting the words "does not include a person" into the definition between the words "hazard" and "means".
f) Damages payable by each and all of the Defendants pursuant to s.24(1) of the Charter to compensate the Plaintiff, vindicate her Charter rights and to deter further infringements of Charter rights;
g) Injunctive relief, including interim, interlocutory and permanent injunctive relief, preventing the Defendants from imposing forced treatment and the Expressive Restrictions;
h) Special damages;
i) Costs, including special costs, elevated costs, and costs on a full indemnity basis;
j) Interest pursuant to the Court Order Interest Act, RSBC 1996, c. 79; and
k) Such further relief as this Court deems just.
[emphasis in original]
[47] These desired remedies do not evidence the plaintiff "being content to 'let the decision[s] stand'" (Myers at para. 33), but represent direct challenges to their legal force and effect. In this, the action as proposed is a thinly-veiled attempt to overturn and avoid the effects of the decisions and actions altogether, in breach of the first TeleZone collateral attack prohibition: see Greengen at paras. 32-33.
[48] As expanded below, the mere insertion of a damages claim will not allow to survive what is in essence an attack on an administrative decision, as illustrated in Stewart. There, the plaintiff brought a civil action against the Superintendent of Pensions arising from the reduction of his retirement pension following the bankruptcy of his former employer. The plaintiff claimed damages against the Superintendent of Pensions and others based in the torts of misfeasance in public office, negligence, and conspiracy with the actuarial firm appointed to administer the wind-up of the plaintiff's pension plan. Greengen explained the Stewart result:
[54] Applying the principles in TeleZone, this Court upheld the dismissal on the basis that the claims in the civil action were a collateral attack on the Superintendent's decision and raised no issues that were distinct and separate from those that were properly the subject of judicial review. Although the plaintiff sought damages as a remedy, the Court determined that his claim was essentially an attack on the lawfulness of the Superintendent's decision that raised issues of jurisdiction, limits of statutory authority, process and fairness. It considered the civil action to be "a claim for judicial review with only a thin pretense to a private wrong" because the plaintiff claimed compensation "for allegedly unlawful conduct of the Superintendent in determining jurisdiction and process." It considered these to be "matters central to the Superintendent's lawful exercise of his powers and to the Court's supervisory jurisdiction by judicial review" (at paras. 77, 79).
[emphasis added]
[49] All of the above emphasised phrases apply to the present claim. The present plaintiff does not accept the defendants' decisions and actions, and seeks various declarations and injunctions that would block or reverse or amend their effects, through judicial interpretations and restrictions of those decisions, in light of their statutory and administrative framework and the Charter. The damages sought do not flow from any specific private cause of action (as the central tort claims of misfeasance in public office in Myers and Greengen) but serve as a punitive add-on to the condemnations and avoidances of those governmental and regulatory decisions and actions that form the subjects of the declarations and other remedies sought.
[50] The constitutional declarations sought also constitute a collateral attack on the decisions and actions of the defendants. They represent an end-run around judicial review. The availability of these remedies on judicial review itself indicates that the declarations should not be granted in this proceeding. In her application response, the plaintiff says that her only purpose for seeking a Charter declaration is to open a "gateway to compensation sought under s. 24(1)". However, a declaration of unconstitutionality of the College and PH Officer's decisions--regardless of the claimed intentions for seeking such a declaration--would lead to the same practical result as the Court quashing the decisions if this proceeding were brought properly as a judicial review.
[51] In Canada (Attorney General) v. Distribution G.V.A. Inc., 2018 FCA 146, Justice Pelletier notes how challenging an administrative decision by way of an action seeking declarations, instead of by way of judicial review, undermined fundamental precepts of administrative law:
[22] In proceeding as it did, GVA bootstrapped itself past the standard of review analysis and imposed a correctness analysis on Health Canada when, as will be seen, the latter would have had the benefit of a reasonableness review had the matter proceeded as an application for judicial review. The issue which this raises is whether a party can, by its choice of remedy, choose the level of scrutiny (standard of review) to which an administrative decision-maker will be held.
[23] In principle and on the basis of this Court's jurisprudence, the answer is no. Accepting that a party can impose a correctness review simply by proceeding by way of declaration would effectively undo decades of administrative law jurisprudence in this Court and in the Supreme Court of Canada to the effect that where Parliament has entrusted the administration of a statutory scheme to an administrative decision-maker, that decision-maker's interpretation of the statutory scheme is entitled to deference: [citations omitted].
[emphasis added]
[52] Our Court of Appeal in Lafontaine v. University of British Columbia, 2018 BCCA 307 similarly denounces such circumvention:
[103] However, "[l]ike other discretionary remedies, declaratory relief should normally be declined where there exists an adequate alternative statutory mechanism to resolve the dispute or to protect the rights in question": Ewert at para. 83. Further, in Gook Country Estates Ltd. v. Quesnel (City of), 2008 BCCA 407, this court held:
[10] When an action is brought by a plaintiff seeking a declaration, the court may deny relief on several discretionary grounds, including standing, delay, mootness, the availability of more appropriate procedures, the absence of affected parties, the theoretical or hypothetical nature of the issue, the inadequacy of the arguments presented, or the fact that the declaration sought is of merely academic importance and has no utility. I do not suggest that this list is exhaustive.
[104] The significance of alternative mechanisms or procedures is particularly pronounced where declarations are sought in ostensible circumvention of a statutory scheme. In those circumstances, issuing declarations has a propensity to conflict with principles underlying curial deference to statutory decision makers: see Dunsmuir v. New Brunswick, 2008 SCC 9 at paras. 48-49. For this reason, they are usually warranted only in "exceptional circumstances": Ewert at paras. 83-84. To this I would add a passage from the dissenting judgement of Justices Côté and Rowe in Ewert, which articulates the risks of issuing declaratory relief outside those "exceptional circumstances":
[127] This Court has stated that "[t]he proper remedy for breach of statutory duty by a public authority, traditionally viewed, is judicial review for invalidity": Holland v. Saskatchewan, 2008 SCC 42, [2008] 2 S.C.R. 551, at para. 9. Allowing inmates to apply to the Federal Court for a declaration that the CSC has violated s. 24 or some other provision would effectively bypass the ordinary process of judicial review. The consequences of a declaratory "bypass" of judicial review are significant. Such a remedy would fail to accord the deference that is typically shown to administrative decision makers. This could open the door to "undue interference with the discharge of administrative functions in respect of the matters delegated to administrative bodies by Parliament and legislatures": Dunsmuir v. New Brunswick, 2008 SCC 9, [2008] 1 S.C.R. 190, at para. 27.
[underlining in original; bold italicised emphasis added]
[53] Lazar Sarna, in The Law of Declaratory Judgments, 4th ed (Toronto: Thomson Reuters Canada Limited, 2016), notes that available alternative proceedings (i.e., judicial review) work to deny declaratory relief in an action:
a) "the power of a court to grant declaratory relief is necessarily ousted where the governing statute provides a special procedure in another forum" (at 51);
b) "the availability of an alternate recourse is sufficient reason to decline the exercise of discretion in favour of permitting declaratory relief" (at 52);
c) "no declaratory proceeding should be entertained if it results in an abuse of process or a procedural or evidentiary prejudice against the other parties to the action" (at 52);
d) "to permit the issuance of a declaration where another suitable remedy exists is to effectively erode the divisions between recourses and subsume all relevant writs and motions under the umbrella of the declaratory proceeding" (at 53); and
e) "[t]o determine that a litigant should have the right to choose a declaratory route over all other equally suitable routes is in some manner to permit the use of a general tool for a specialized function and ultimately to permit the abandonment of the special tools available" (at 53).
[54] Moving from the relief sought to the claims advanced, the present purported causes of action do not raise issues that are distinct from those that would be addressed on judicial review. Northern Cross reviews and distinguishes the causes of action advanced in cases wherein the claims survived a collateral attack strike application:
[58] In my view, aspects of the blunt propositions that are advanced by Yukon do not accord with TeleZone. Nevertheless, there are two interrelated difficulties with Chance's "unlawful de facto cancellation" claim. First, an "unlawful de facto cancellation" is not an existing cause of action. In TeleZone, and in, for example, Nu‑Pharm, Parrish and Manuge, the Court permitted various civil actions to proceed without first requiring the plaintiffs in those actions to seek judicial review, but these were all based on existing causes of actions. I have earlier identified the causes of action that were advanced in TeleZone. In Nu‑Pharm, the plaintiff sought damages for illegal interference with economic interests, abuse of authority, misfeasance of public office and negligence. Similar causes of action were advanced in Parrish. In Manuge, the plaintiff brought a class action seeking constitutional remedies, declarations, damages or restitution and a claim for breach of fiduciary duty.
[55] Northern Cross explains, in contrast, why the claims in Union Road Properties Ltd. v. British Columbia (Agricultural Land Commission), 2019 BCCA 302 were properly struck:
[65] In Union Road Properties Ltd. v. British Columbia (Agricultural Land Commission), 2019 BCCA 302, the appellants similarly had their pleadings struck for failing to disclose a cause of action. The Court concluded, after referring to TeleZone and reviewing the pleadings, that all of the wrongs pleaded were directly related to the manner in which the Commission had performed its statutory powers and obligations: at paras. 16-17.
[56] The present Court must thus consider whether the plaintiff's claims "[allege] the elements of a private cause of action" or whether the "essential character" of those claims mirror an application for judicial review: Northern Cross at para. 68, citing TeleZone at paras. 76, 78.
[57] In TeleZone, the Court permitted the action to continue on the basis that the plaintiff did not seek to avoid the effects of the administrative decisions in question, but rather cited their purported unlawfulness in advancing private law claims--specifically, in negligence, breach of contract, and unjust enrichment: see para. 1.
[58] In contrast, here, the NCC advance no such private claims, and the PANCC, as set out below, adds on a single defective tort claim in intimidation that is bound to fail on the plaintiff's own pleaded factual foundation.
[59] I reach a conclusion similar to that in Northern Cross at para. 68. The pleading at its core--its "essential character"--is directed to the legality of the defendants' decisions and actions, in the exercise of their statutory powers and obligations, and mirrors an application for judicial review. The causes of action advanced in the pleadings raise issues that are precisely the kinds of issues that would be addressed on judicial review: Northern Cross at para. 61. The plaintiff is not content to let the administrative decisions stand, but is expressly seeking to reverse and avoid any effects of those actions. Here, as there, the claim has no reasonable prospect of success and should be struck.
2. Remedies available on judicial review
[60] The plaintiff argues that judicial review would be inappropriate and ineffective, as a court would be unable to provide some of the remedies that she seeks:
a) damages;
b) an order rendering portions of the PH Act of no force and effect pursuant to s. 52 of the Constitution Act, 1982; and
c) an order "reading in" or "reading down" the PH Act pursuant to s. 24(1) of the Charter.
[61] As indicated by Stewart, above, and Ernst v. Alberta Energy Regulator, 2017 SCC 1, below, the inability to seek certain remedies on judicial review does not in itself relieve the claimant from proceeding by way of judicial review if that is the appropriate judicial avenue. For example, the fact that the Ernst plaintiff was seeking damages did not insulate him from the Court's confirmation that judicial review was the appropriate avenue to pursue his grievances.
[62] As argued by the College, "the remedy 'tail' cannot wag the liability 'dog'". In the words of Johnson v. MacDougall, 2019 BCSC 743 at para. 30, rev'd on other grounds 2022 BCCA 82, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 40174 (16 February 2023), the mere seeking of Charter damages "does not bulletproof a claim from a motion to strike" where it is appropriately brought by way of judicial review. Scow recently compellingly stated this principle:
[90] I reject the plaintiff's contention that the nature of the relief sought--equitable damages in an amount equal to the total tax improperly assessed or, alternatively, restitution of the total amounts improperly assessed--gives the Supreme Court jurisdiction to decide Claim 2. To suggest that the nature of the remedy sought determines the jurisdiction of a court to entertain a claim is to put the remedial cart before the subject matter jurisdiction horse. The first question is whether the court in which the proceeding is brought has jurisdiction over the subject matter of the claim. If it does not, the availability of the remedies sought is irrelevant.
[emphasis added]
[63] McLean v. Law Society of British Columbia, 2017 BCSC 987 at paras. 77-79 similarly confirms that a complaint in essence stemming from dissatisfaction with administrative decisions and actions must proceed by way of judicial review, notwithstanding a pleaded claim for Charter damages. Justice Grauer (then of this Court) states as follows:
[79] In my view, the same can be said of this case. Even though Mr. McLean claims damages for breach of his Charter rights and his privacy rights, and in tort, his claim fundamentally rests on the assertions that the investigation order was ultra vires the Law Society and was made in a manner that breached the decision-maker's duty of fairness. Indeed his claim for damages for the tort of conspiracy, as he framed it before me, rests entirely on the asserted unlawfulness of the investigation order. Hence the relief Mr. McLean seeks in the form of a declaration that the investigation order is void and an order setting it aside. These would constitute an order for certiorari, which the Judicial Review Procedure Act, RSBC 1996, c 241, requires be brought by petition.
[emphasis added]
[64] In any case, the plaintiff's assertion is incorrect with respect to the ability of a court to grant constitutional remedies on a judicial review. As a matter of constitutional law, a section 96 court of inherent jurisdiction maintains a supervisory authority over administrative decision-makers, and has the inherent power to grant constitutional remedies such as those sought by the plaintiff: Professor Kent Roach, Constitutional Remedies in Canada, at § 2:10, 11:19, 13:25.[6] While courts will generally not grant constitutional remedies in judicial review proceedings where the matter was not raised before the decision-maker in the first instance, this is not because a section 96 court lacks the power to do so. With an eye to the judicial restraint necessarily applied in reviewing an administrative decision or action, courts generally require constitutional questions to be first raised before administrative decision-makers, the disposition of which will form a key part of the record for the reviewing court: Denton v. British Columbia (Workers' Compensation Appeal Tribunal), 2017 BCCA 403 at paras. 48-52, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 37923 (25 October 2018).
[65] Cases in which courts have decided the constitutionality of legislation within a judicial review include British Columbia (Environmental Management Act) v. Canadian National Railway Company, 2022 BCSC 135; T.L. v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2023 BCCA 167; Klassen v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2021 BCSC 2254; Gregory v. British Columbia (Superintendent of Motor Vehicles), 2016 BCSC 1192, aff'd 2018 BCCA 7.
[66] Courts commonly consider in the context of a judicial review allegations that administrative actions have breached the Charter rights of petitioners, including in the Covid context, as illustrated in the list of cases footnoted in the introduction to these reasons. Recently, in the Covid context, our Court of Appeal in Beaudoin at para. 258 confirms that judicial review engaging Charter concerns will apply a "robust proportionality analysis" of the administrative decisions, as established in Doré v. Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12 and affirmed in Loyola High School. As stated in Vavilov:
[57] Although the amici questioned the approach to the standard of review set out in Doré v. Barreau du Québec, 2012 SCC 12, [2012] 1 S.C.R. 395, a reconsideration of that approach is not germane to the issues in this appeal. However, it is important to draw a distinction between cases in which it is alleged that the effect of the administrative decision being reviewed is to unjustifiably limit rights under the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms (as was the case in Doré) and those in which the issue on review is whether a provision of the decision maker's enabling statute violates the Charter (see, e.g., Nova Scotia (Workers' Compensation Board) v. Martin, 2003 SCC 54, [2003] 2 S.C.R. 504, at para. 65). Our jurisprudence holds that an administrative decision maker's interpretation of the latter issue should be reviewed for correctness, and that jurisprudence is not displaced by these reasons.
[67] Similarly, Hoogerbrug recently found that PH Orders against Covid did not offend those petitioners' rights under ss. 2(a) or 7 of the Charter: paras 17, 18, 294, 314. That decision conducted an extensive review of whether the PH Orders infringed the constitutional rights of the petitioners: see paras. 233-314.
[68] Superior provincial courts may also apply constitutional remedies in such contexts. Kaps v. City of Surrey, 2022 BCSC 1191 provides a recent example. The petitioners brought a judicial review of a municipal by-law, claiming that it restricted their s. 2(b) expressive rights. The municipality failed in its onus of justifying the infringement. The Court considered its potential s. 52 constitutional remedies. Rather than striking down the law (in itself of course another constitutional remedy), the Court exercised the remedy of reading in additions to the by-law that would avoid the Charter breach.
C. Subrule (a): discloses no reasonable claim
1. Law
[69] The test under Rule 9-5(1)(a) is whether, assuming the pleaded facts to be true, it is plain and obvious the claim will not succeed. The court is reluctant to deprive a party of the right to pursue a claim unless it is "plain and obvious" that the plaintiff's pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action. As stated in Imperial Tobacco:
[17] A claim will only be struck if it is plain and obvious, assuming the facts pleaded to be true, that the pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action: [citations omitted]. Another way of putting the test is that the claim has no reasonable prospect of success. Where a reasonable prospect of success exists, the matter should be allowed to proceed to trial: [citations omitted].
[70] As Hunt v. Carey Canada Inc., [1990] 2 SCR 959 at 980 states with respect to Rule 19(24)(a), the predecessor of Rule 9-5(1)(a):
Thus, the test in Canada governing the application of provisions like Rule 19(24)(a) of the British Columbia Rules of Court is the same as the one that governs an application under R.S.C. O. 18, r. 19: assuming that the facts as stated in the statement of claim can be proved, is it "plain and obvious" that the plaintiff's statement of claim discloses no reasonable cause of action? As in England, if there is a chance that the plaintiff might succeed, then the plaintiff should not be "driven from the judgment seat". Neither the length and complexity of the issues, the novelty of the cause of action, nor the potential for the defendant to present a strong defence should prevent the plaintiff from proceeding with his or her case. Only if the action is certain to fail because it contains a radical defect ranking with the others listed in Rule 19(24) of the British Columbia Rules of Court should the relevant portions of a plaintiff's statement of claim be struck out under Rule 19(24)(a).
[emphasis added]
[71] In Citizens for Foreign Aid Reform Inc. v. Canadian Jewish Congress (1999), 36 CPC (4th) 266 (BCSC), the late Mr Justice Romilly summarises the principles governing a strike application:
34 To succeed on an application under Rule 19(24) (a) it must be established that it is "plain and obvious" that the pleading discloses no reasonable cause of action: Hunt v. T & N plc, [1990] 2 S.C.R. 959 (S.C.C.). If there is any doubt it should be resolved in favour of permitting the pleadings to stand: McGauley v. British Columbia (1989), 39 B.C.L.R. (2d) 223 (B.C.C.A.). The court should proceed on the assumption that all the facts pled are true: Helman v. Brown (1966), 57 W.W.R. 608 (B.C. C.A.). The only question then is whether the facts disclose a cause of action: Minnes v. Minnes (1962), 39 W.W.R. 112 (B.C. C.A.); McNaughton v. Baker (1988), 25 B.C.L.R. (2d) 17 (B.C. C.A.). In other words, as long as the pleadings disclose a triable issue, either as it exists, or as it may be amended, then the issue should go to trial. The mere fact that the case is weak or not likely to succeed is no ground for striking it out under the provisions of Rule 19(24): Minnes, supra.
[72] Despite this generous approach, the court is not required to assume that all allegations and assumptions are true. Rather, it may be appropriate to subject them to a "sceptical analysis": Young v. Borzoni et al, 2007 BCCA 16 at paras. 30-32. Further, a plaintiff "is not entitled to rely on the possibility that new facts may turn up as the case progresses": Imperial Tobacco at para. 22.
[73] No special consideration is given for cases in which the plaintiff alleges Charter infringements: Scott v. Canada (Attorney General), 2013 BCSC 1651 at para. 21, rev'd on other grounds 2017 BCCA 422, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 37930 (30 August, 2018), citing Canadian Bar Association v. British Columbia, 2008 BCCA 92 at para. 51, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 32600 (31 July 2008).
[74] Applications for leave to amend pleadings are considered on the same basis as applications to strike pleadings with the question being whether it is plain and obvious that the proposed amendments are bound to fail. In assessing that question, it is not determinative that the law has not yet recognised a particular claim. The court must be generous in its analysis, and err on the side of permitting an arguable claim to proceed to trial: British Columbia (Director of Civil Forfeiture) v. Violette, 2015 BCSC 1372 at para. 40.
2. Discussion and decision
a) Claims based on unlikely hypothetical future events
[75] The bulk of the plaintiff's claim is based upon a hypothetical future event that will likely never happen. As set out in paras 1:24 and 1:32 of the NCC and 1:39 of the PANCC:
24. On the basis of the October 16, 2021 notice, the February 9, 2022 press release, and the PHO order issued March 7, 2022, the Plaintiff anticipates that she will be forced by the PHO and/or the College to choose between practicing her profession and her patients' well-being and being vaccinated with an mRNA therapy or other vaccine authorized by the Health Canada on an emergency basis.
..
Overbreadth
32. The Plaintiff anticipates that the public health order will not provide an exemption from forced treatment for persons, such as herself, who have acquired natural immunity as a result of prior infection with Covid-19, or those who are practicing medicine remotely by videoconferencing or tele-practice, or those who are not at significant risk of hospitalization or serious symptoms or death, or those practitioners who are willing to self-administer routine rapid testing, or those who wear N95 or equivalent masks in well-ventilated environments.
.
Reasonable Apprehension of Breach of Charter Right
39. The Plaintiff's apprehension of a reasonably imminent breach of her Charter right to bodily autonomy and medical decisional autonomy is empirically based and is reasonable. The apprehension is not speculative. Given the PHO and Minister's repeated assertions of their intention to impose forced treatment on the Plaintiff, it would be unreasonable to deprive the Plaintiff of her right to assert her constitutional rights until after the Defendants have forced treatment on her without her consent or have deprived her of her entitlement to work as a medical professional.
[underlining in original; bold italicised emphasis added]
[76] The plaintiff's claim is based upon a double hypothetical: that the defendants will issue a mandatory vaccination order, and that the mandatory vaccination order will provide no exemptions. The potential for exemptions to such a hypothetical order, or the wording of the hypothetical order itself, piles further hypotheticals on further hypotheticals.
[77] Again, even during the height of the pandemic, neither the PH Officer nor the College ever required naturopath doctors to be vaccinated, generally, or as a prerequisite to practice (in contrast to the effect of the orders considered in Hoogerbrug). The defendants have never, in contemplation or reality, to use the repeated phrase in the NCC and PANCC, "forced" the plaintiff to submit to vaccination or other medical treatment.
[78] The Court cannot assume as a fact that the PH Officer will make an administrative order in the future requiring the plaintiff to be vaccinated, and that the order will not contain appropriate Charter protections and/or exemptions. The plaintiff has not pleaded and cannot plead any material facts that would support this claim. Rather, the plaintiff has drawn speculative inferences incapable of proof from existing administrative orders and public statements by the PH Officer. Such pleadings do not constitute material facts, and will be struck: Kindylides v. Does, 2020 BCCA 330 at para. 33, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 39728 (14 October 2021).
[79] Even if one assumes, arguendo, that the facts pleaded in the NCC and PANCC are true, however, they still disclose no reasonable cause of action. As no such requirement has been imposed, and likely never will be imposed, the issues are unripe for adjudication. As a matter of judicial economy, in a time of scarce judicial resources, courts do not adjudicate unripe and premature claims, or consider claims for the sole purpose of remedying only hypothetical disputes: Operation Dismantle v. The Queen, [1985] 1 SCR 441 at 447-48. A claim will not be ripe for adjudication if it rests upon contingent future events that may not occur as anticipated, or at all: Atkins v. Anmore (Village), 2014 BCSC 2402 at para. 45.
[80] This principle applies to causes of action, as well as relief sought in the form of declarations and injunctions. A court will not take remedial action where the occurrence of future harm is not probable: Operation Dismantle at 458. A declaration may only be granted if it will settle a "live controversy" between the parties: Kraft Heinz Canada ULC v. Canada (Attorney General), 2022 BCSC 796 at para. 34.
[81] While a declaration may be issued to affect future rights, it must be based on more than mere hypothetical consequences: there must be a cognisable threat to a legal interest before the courts will entertain the use of its process as a preventive measure: Operation Dismantle at 457. Similarly, there must be a "very strong probability" that "grave" harm will in fact occur, before the court will issue a quia timet prospective injunction: Operation Dismantle at 457-58.
[82] The respondent cites New Brunswick (Minister of Health and Community Services) v. G.(J.), [1999] 3 SCR 46 for the proposition that a court can order remedies for anticipated breach of Charter rights. That decision, however, illustrates the restrained approach set out above: it considered a live controversy. The Court had "little doubt" that state removal of the applicants' children constituted a "serious interference with the[ir] psychological integrity", which triggered liberty interests under s. 7 of the Charter: para. 61. Further, that state removal was not distant or hypothetical: the Ministry had already obtained and then applied for an extension of that state removal. In this context of ongoing removal of the children, the Court confirmed that parents had a constitutional right to state-funded legal aid in such hearings.
[83] All of these principles are amplified where the claim, as here, is based upon alleged Charter breaches. Without a specific governmental enactment, how can the Court assess whether the hypothetical public health order will be overbroad ? (as the plaintiff asks in her PANCC para 1:32, quoted above). How can the Court carry out a s. 1 Charter analysis of whether the hypothetical restrictions or mandates can be justified in a free and democratic society? (as anticipated in paras. 1:34-38 and 3:19 of the PANCC). Neither exercise is possible in the hypothetical. As stated in Mackay v. Manitoba, [1989] 2 SCR 357 at 361, 366:
Charter decisions should not and must not be made in a factual vacuum. To attempt to do so would trivialize the Charter and inevitably result in ill-considered opinions. The presentation of facts is not, as stated by the respondent, a mere technicality; rather, it is essential to a proper consideration of Charter issues. A respondent cannot, by simply consenting to dispense with the factual background, require or expect a court to deal with an issue such as this in a factual void. Charter decisions cannot be based upon the unsupported hypotheses of enthusiastic counsel.
.
A factual foundation is of fundamental importance on this appeal. It is not the purpose of the legislation which is said to infringe the Charter but its effects. If the deleterious effects are not established there can be no Charter violation and no case has been made out. Thus the absence of a factual base is not just a technicality that could be overlooked, but rather it is a flaw that is fatal to the appellants' position.[7]
b) Challenged emergency health orders now judicially confirmed after review
[84] Even in the unlikely event that the hypothetical became real, and the PH Officer required the plaintiff and her colleagues to be vaccinated, Hoogerbrug has recently confirmed that the PH Officer's continued use of emergency powers under the PH Act, including the actual imposition of a vaccination requirement for health care workers, was justified as of October 5, 2023: the date that requirement was renewed and continued. The Hoogerbrug petitioners (in three consolidated petitions) were healthcare workers who had in fact been terminated from their jobs for remaining unvaccinated in the face of an actual PH vaccination requirement. The petitioners challenged the PH Orders on administrative law and Charter grounds, arguing that the Orders constituted an unreasonable exercise of the PH Officer's powers under the PH Act, and that they unjustifiably infringed ss. 2(a) and 7 of the Charter. The Court dismissed the petition:
[198] In sum, in light of the record as of October 5, 2023 and applying the appropriate legal deference, in my view the record supported the reasonableness of the PHO's decision to continue to exercise the emergency powers in the PHA, to protect public health, and in particular the functioning of the healthcare system and the health of those most vulnerable to COVID-19.
[209] In sum, I find the PHO's decisions about the risks of unvaccinated healthcare workers to be reasonable. The record provided ample support for the reasonableness of her Conclusions (quoted in paragraph 62 above) that, at the time of the Orders: unvaccinated healthcare workers continued to pose unacceptable risks to vulnerable patients and other healthcare workers; and, it was therefore essential to maintain the high level of vaccination in place in the hospital, community care and residential care workforce, to mitigate the risks to patients and other healthcare workers, and to safeguard the functioning of the healthcare system.
[85] In their supplemental written submissions, the defendants argue that this conclusion constitutes stare decisis binding upon this dispute. While it is a matter of stare decisis, that conclusion was reached within the deferential context of a judicial review, and thus notionally would not determine the outcome of an action, as proposed here. That said, it would weigh heavily in the consideration of this dispute were it to proceed to trial, and in any case, this claim, is, as set out above, properly the subject of a judicial review rather than a challenge to hypothetical administrative decisions and actions cleverly drafted in the form of an action.
c) Proposed new section 7 claims
[86] The above problems also speak to the plaintiff's present and proposed claims and remedies based on alleged breaches of s. 7 of the Charter (para. 1:39), quoted above, as well as a proposed amendment asserting that "the Defendants have violated her right to liberty and security of the person under s.7 of the Charter by imposing upon her serious and significant psychological stress by means of ongoing threats of fines, imprisonment and deprivation of her livelihood and professional status" (para. 3:27). For those actions, along with the proposed new intimidation claim, the plaintiff "seeks monetary compensation for state imposed stress and intimidation" [underlining in original, to denote proposed amendments].
[87] The underlying basis for the Charter damages based on breaches of ss. 2(b) and 7 is on its face untenable:
Loss of Business, Loss of Revenue
51. The Plaintiff has lost business as a result of the Expressive Restrictions. A number of the Plaintiff's existing patients have requested that she provide them with advice regarding natural immunity and immune health as it relates to Covid-19. The Expressive Restrictions have prevented the Plaintiff from providing her patients with medical advice in those areas. As a result, the Plaintiff has lost revenue and seeks compensation for business losses from the College, the PHO and the Minister of Health, all of which have contributed to the implementation of the Expressive Restrictions.
52. The Plaintiff has lost revenue as a result of the anticipated breach of her right to liberty and security of the person. Were it not for the ongoing threats to compel her to cease practice or submit to vaccination, the Plaintiff would have expanded her practice to open a clinic and/or obtain office space by joining another clinic and thereby increase her revenue. Due to the threats of forced vaccination and revocation of her entitlement to practice naturopathic medicine, she suffered significant psychological stress and uncertainty about future revenues and she was unable to expand her practice. The Plaintiff seeks compensation for past and future business losses.
[emphasis in original, to denote proposed amendment]
[88] Section 7 does not bestow upon individuals an unconstrained right to transact business whenever or however one wishes: R. v. Edwards Books and Art Ltd., [1986] 2 SCR 713 at 785-86. The Charter also does not confer a "constitutional right to practise a profession unfettered by the applicable rules and standards which regulate that profession": see Mussani v. College of Physicians and Surgeons of Ontario (2004), 74 OR (3d) 1 (CA) at paras. 41, 43; Hoogerbrug at paras 18, 277-278; 281-86 (and cited authorities). State action that limits or puts conditions on a person's ability to practise a profession engages only "an economic interest of the sort that is not protected by the Charter": see Mussani at paras. 41, 43; see also Cambie Surgeries Corporation v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2020 BCSC 1310 at para. 1766, aff'd 2022 BCCA 245 at paras. 233-235, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 40412 (6 April 2023).
[89] In the specific context of the pandemic, Courts have repeatedly confirmed that a requirement to be vaccinated in order to practice one's profession does not amount to "forced vaccination", or violate informed consent or bodily autonomy, or violate Charter rights: Maddock v. British Columbia, 2022 BCSC 1605 at paras. 78-79, 83, appeal dismissed as moot, Kassian v. British Columbia, 2023 BCCA 383; Parmar v. Tribe Management Inc., 2022 BCSC 1675 at paras. 132-33, 153-56; Amalgamated Transit Union, Local 113 et al v. Toronto Transit Commission and National Organized Workers Union v. Sinai Health System, 2021 ONSC 7658 at paras. 50-52, aff'd 2022 ONCA 802. The recent words in Hoogerbrug speak equally to this case:
[276] On the evidence, the Orders compelled none of the Tatlock petitioners to accept unwanted medical treatment. Thus, unlike Carter, their s. 7 rights associated with bodily integrity and medical self-determination were not engaged.
[277] Instead, they lost their jobs because they chose not to accept vaccination against a highly contagious virus which posed the risk of serious illness and death to vulnerable patients and other healthcare workers. In my view, this loss did not engage their s. 7 right to liberty because of the well-established principle that s. 7 does not protect the right to work in any specific employment or particular profession, particularly when the job-loss arises from non-compliance with its governing rules and regulations. This is not a constitutionally-protected fundamental life choice.
[278] In my view, their s. 7 security of the person rights were also not engaged. The fact that they experienced serious consequences, including stress and hardship, from choosing to follow their personal convictions about vaccination does not make the Orders a state interference with their physical or psychological integrity. In effect, their position amounts to security of the person being engaged unless vaccination were a matter of free choice without any serious state-imposed consequences for refusal. As stated in by Chief Justice Lamer in J.G. at para. 59:
It is clear that the right to security of the person does not protect the individual from the ordinary stresses and anxieties that a person of reasonable sensibility would suffer as a result of government action. If the right were interpreted with such broad sweep, countless government initiatives could be challenged on the ground that they infringe the right to security of the person, massively expanding the scope of judicial review, and, in the process, trivializing what it means for a right to be constitutionally protected
[Emphasis in original]
[90] While a hypothetical health order, in the unlikely event that it does come to pass, may perhaps force the plaintiff to make a difficult decision, depending on the wording and terms of the order, and whether it allowed exemptions, its justiciability, in all likelihood, would be barred by the authorities above.
[91] Further, the proposed claim in itself runs up against the plaintiff's own cited case of New Brunswick (Minister of Health and Community Services) casting doubt on such a claim:
59 Delineating the boundaries protecting the individual's psychological integrity from state interference is an inexact science. Dickson C.J. in Morgentaler, supra, at p. 56, suggested that security of the person would be restricted through "serious state-imposed psychological stress" (emphasis added). Dickson C.J. was trying to convey something qualitative about the type of state interference that would rise to the level of an infringement of this right. It is clear that the right to security of the person does not protect the individual from the ordinary stresses and anxieties that a person of reasonable sensibility would suffer as a result of government action. If the right were interpreted with such broad sweep, countless government initiatives could be challenged on the ground that they infringe the right to security of the person, massively expanding the scope of judicial review, and, in the process, trivializing what it means for a right to be constitutionally protected. Nor will every violation of a fundamental freedom guaranteed in s. 2 of the Charter amount to a restriction of security of the person. I do not believe it can be seriously argued that a law prohibiting certain kinds of commercial expression in violation of s. 2(b), for example, will necessarily result in a violation of the psychological integrity of the person. This is not to say, though, that there will never be cases where a violation of s. 2 will also deprive an individual of security of the person.
[underlining in original; bold italicized emphasis added]
[92] As stated in TeleZone at para. 69: "Governments make discretionary decisions all the time which will inflict losses on people or businesses without conferring any cause of action known to the law."
[93] The plaintiff does not cite any cases where courts have awarded Charter damages for alleged breaches of ss. 2(b) or 7 based in whole or in part on interference with the plaintiff's business, and no precedent is apparent in the jurisprudence. In the present circumstances, given the manifold difficulties with the claim reviewed above, the tenuous nature of this novel claim should work towards striking the claim, rather than allowing its survival based on mere novelty alone. As stated in Atlantic Lottery Corp. Inc. v. Babstock, 2020 SCC 19:
[19] Of course, it is not determinative on a motion to strike that the law has not yet recognized the particular claim. The law is not static, and novel claims that might represent an incremental development in the law should be allowed to proceed to trial That said, a claim will not survive an application to strike simply because it is novel. It is beneficial, and indeed critical to the viability of civil justice and public access thereto that claims, including novel claims, which are doomed to fail be disposed of at an early stage in the proceedings. This is because such claims present "no legal justification for a protracted and expensive trial" If a court would not recognize a novel claim when the facts as pleaded are taken to be true, the claim is plainly doomed to fail and should be struck. In making this determination, it is not uncommon for courts to resolve complex questions of law and policy...
[authorities omitted]
d) Proposed new intimidation claim
[94] In further response to the strike application, the PANCC inserts a brief new claim of intimidation, at para. 3:27:
Furthermore, each of the Defendants are liable to the Plaintiff for committing the common law tort of intimidation by repeatedly demanding that she submit to unwanted vaccine treatment and backing that demand by coercive and unlawful threats. The Plaintiff seeks monetary compensation for state imposed stress and intimidation.
[emphasis in original]
[95] The proposed new intimidation claim is improperly pleaded, and is, in any case, bound to fail.
[96] Central Canada Potash Co. Ltd. et al. v. Government of Saskatchewan, [1979] 1 SCR 42 at 81 describes the tort of intimidation:
A commits a tort if he delivers a threat to B that he will commit an act or use means unlawful as against B, as a result of which B does or refrains from doing some act which he is entitled to do, thereby causing damage either to himself or to C. The tort is one of intention and the plaintiff, whether it be B or C, must be a person whom A intended to injure.
[97] The essential elements of the tort of intimidation are thus:
a) the defendant threatens unlawful acts in order to coerce the plaintiff into doing or not doing certain acts;
b) the defendant intends that the plaintiff will suffer injury as a result;
c) the plaintiff submits to the threat by doing or not doing certain acts; and
d) as a result, the plaintiff suffers actual damage.[8]
[98] With respect to the first element, the essence of the tort of intimidation is the threat of an unlawful act: Philip H Osborne, The Law of Torts, 6th ed (Toronto: Irwin Law, 2020) at 349; Lewis N Klar & Cameron SG Jefferies, Tort Law, 6th ed (Toronto: Thomson Reuters, 2017) at 832. As indicated by the first requirement, the tort of intimidation does not arise where a defendant has followed a course of action that they believe to be lawful: Sauvé v. Canada, 2011 FC 1074 at para. 44, aff'd 2012 FCA 280; White Hatter Limousine Service Ltd. v. Calgary (City), [1994] 1 WWR 620 (Alt KB) at para. 28. For example, the tort of intimidation is not made out where "a party to a contract asserts what he reasonably considers to be his contractual right": Central Canada Potash at 87. Specifically, a governmental defendant is entitled to enforce a regulation until and unless it is found to be unlawful, absent which that enforcement cannot constitute intimidation: Central Canada Potash at 90. Conversely, "if the course of conduct which the person making the threat seeks to induce is that which the person threatened is obligated to follow, the tort of intimidation does not arise": Central Canada Potash at 46.
[99] With respect to the second element, "[t]he tort is one of intention": Central Canada Potash at 81. Here, the plaintiff fails to plead that the defendants intended to injure the plaintiff. It is difficult to conceive how she could prove that assertion.
[100] With respect to the third element, unless the threatened plaintiff submits to the threat and changes their actions accordingly, there can be no claim in intimidation: Skybridge Investments Ltd. v.
