Metro Motors Ltd., 2006 BCCA 500 at paras. 28-29; Vancouver Museums & Planetarium Assn. v. V.M.R.E.U. (1981), 27 BCLR 73 (CA); Robin Hood Management Ltd v. Gelmich, 2016 SKQB 279 at paras. 23-25; Pollock v. The Government of Manitoba, 2005 MBCA 31 at paras. 11, 15.
[101] The plaintiff fails to plead that she has submitted to the alleged threat. On the contrary, she pleads that "she has not received the Pfizer/Moderna therapy or been treated with any other vaccine against Covid-19 authorised by Health Canada on an emergency basis": para. 1:1. In other words, she expressly pleads that she has not "submitted" to the alleged "threat" requiring her vaccination.
[102] As pleaded, the proposed new tort is bound to fail and will not be permitted as an amendment.
e) Damages claims
i. Introduction
[103] The plaintiff's damages claims, in any pleaded formulation, are also bound to fail, for multiple reasons.
[104] The NCC does not plead material facts or a legal basis for an award of private law damages, including special damages. As set out above, the PANCC intimidation claim is improperly pleaded and untenable.
[105] As further set out above, with respect to the Charter s. 7 claim, the PH Officer has not issued any order requiring the plaintiff to be vaccinated. The claim is entirely hypothetical, and thus provides no basis for Charter damages.
ii. PH Act s. 92 immunity provision
[106] Further, s. 92 of the PH Act bars both private law and Charter damages claims against the PH Officer:
Immunity from legal proceedings
92 (1) Subject to subsection (2), no legal proceeding for damages lies or may be commenced or maintained against a health officer, a commissioner under Division 1 [Inquiries] or a person acting under the order or direction of either of these, because of anything done or omitted
(a) in the exercise or intended exercise of a power under this or any other enactment, or
(b) in the performance or intended performance of a duty under this or any other enactment.
(2) Subsection (1) does not apply to a person referred to in that subsection in relation to anything done or omitted in bad faith.
(3) Subsection (1) does not absolve the government or a health authority from vicarious liability arising out of anything done or omitted by a person referred to in that subsection for which the government or health authority would be vicariously liable if this section were not in force.
[107] In a strike application, the court should consider and give considerable weight to such immunity provisions. If it is plain and obvious that an immunity applies in light of the facts pleaded, the claim should be struck: Ernst at paras. 19 (per Cromwell J), 67 (per Abella J, concurring), 149 (per McLachlin CJ dissenting); Walkom v. Law Society of British Columbia, 2019 BCCA 391 at para. 19.
[108] In Ernst, the Supreme Court of Canada confirms the efficacy of such immunity clauses, striking a claim seeking Charter damages for alleged breaches of the plaintiff's s. 2(b) expression rights by the Alberta Energy Regulator. The Court notes that such clauses should be read expansively, to protect administrative decision-makers from the distraction of litigation flowing from the decisions they make in their legislatively-designated roles:
[57] Immunity is easily frustrated where the mere pleading of an allegation of bad faith or punitive conduct in a statement of claim can call into question a decision-maker's conduct: Gonzalez, at para. 53. Even qualified immunity undermines the decision-maker's ability to act impartially and independently, as the mere threat of litigation, achieved by artful pleadings, will require the decision-maker to engage with claims brought against him or her. As Lord Denning M.R. held, to be truly free in thought, judges should not be "plagued with allegations of malice or ill-will or bias or anything of the kind": Sirros, at p. 136, cited by Morier, at pp. 739-40.
[109] It is conceded that the Ernst Court was considering the Alberta Energy Regulator, serving a quasi-adjudicative function, with an ostensibly absolute immunity clause. But these words also speak for the most part to the present defendants, legislatively entrusted to carry out their important public functions, here as well as below, in the discussion of factors countervailing against the justice and appropriateness of a Charter damages claim. As noted by the Ernst Court, the immunity clause, there, as here, does not bar judicial oversight and intervention by way of judicial review; it only bars damages claims: para. 33.
[110] The quotation above also indicates that a court should be sceptical and wary of artful pleadings of bad faith against defendants in order to avoid an immunity clause, as here.
[111] Section 92 is drafted in the broadest possible terms. There is no pleaded allegation that the PH Officer is acting in any other capacity than "in the exercise or intended exercise of a power under this or any other enactment" (s. 92(1)(a)), and it is difficult to conceive how one could plead or prove such a proposition.
[112] In Hart v. Henry, 2021 BCPC 249 at paras. 13-15, the Court specifically dismissed a claim seeking damages against the PH Officer flowing from a public health order imposing restrictions on restaurants, on the basis of s. 92. The result equally applies to bar the present claim.
iii. Charter damages are neither appropriate nor just
[113] Ernst and its jurisprudence also confirm that a court hearing a strike application must consider whether Charter damages are appropriate and just and, if they are not, strike the claim. In Ernst, both the majority (paras. 26, 41) and dissent decisions (paras. 161-162, 165) confirm the appropriateness of striking a claim where judicial review would be an alternative and more effective remedy than would be Charter damages. Our Court of Appeal has also recently and repeatedly confirmed the appropriateness of striking an inappropriate claim for Charter damages: Quinn at para. 45-73; Johnson v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2022 BCCA 82 at paras. 55-60, lv to appeal to SCC ref'd, 40174 (16 February 2023); Workers' Compensation Board v. Sort, 2022 BCCA 318 at paras. 89-92.
[114] Ernst tempered the availability of Charter damages, as set out in Vancouver (City) v. Ward, 2010 SCC 27, the case most heavily relied upon by the present plaintiff. Justice Cromwell, writing for the majority, at para. 25 quoted the following from Henry v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2015 SCC 24 at para. 91: "when it comes to awarding Charter damages, courts must be careful not to extend their availability too far." Justice Cromwell further reminded that:
[26] Not every bare allegation claiming Charter damages must proceed to an individualized, case-by-case consideration on its particular merits. Ward held that Charter damages will not be an appropriate and just remedy where there is an effective alternative remedy or where damages would be contrary to the demands of good governance. These considerations, taken together, support the conclusion that the proper balance would be struck by holding that damages are not an appropriate remedy.
[emphasis added]
[115] In Ward, the Supreme Court held that in order to determine whether damages are appropriate and just in the circumstances of a particular case, a four-step inquiry must be conducted:
[4] The first step in the inquiry is to establish that a Charter right has been breached. The second step is to show why damages are a just and appropriate remedy, having regard to whether they would fulfill one or more of the related functions of compensation, vindication of the right, and/or deterrence of future breaches. At the third step, the state has the opportunity to demonstrate, if it can, that countervailing factors defeat the functional considerations that support a damage award and render damages inappropriate or unjust. The final step is to assess the quantum of the damages.
[116] The plaintiff bears the burden of establishing a prima facie case by establishing the first two steps. If established, the onus shifts to the defendant to demonstrate that countervailing considerations render damages inappropriate or unjust. In the context of an application to strike under subrule 9-5(1)(a), the court must assess whether the plaintiff's claim has a "reasonable prospect of success" at each of the steps set out in Ward: Quinn at para. 49.
[117] Here, as in Quinn and Ernst, the claim founders on the third enquiry: countervailing factors--specifically the availability and greater appropriateness of other avenues--defeat any functional considerations that would support a damage award, and render damages inappropriate and unjust.
[118] Ernst notes that Charter damages may be considered inappropriate where more effective avenues--such as judicial review--exist, or where allowance of Charter damages would interfere with concerns for effective governance:
[27] Section 24(1) of the Charter confers on the courts a broad remedial authority. As has been said, "[i]t is difficult to imagine . . . a wider and less fettered discretion": Mills v. The Queen, [1986] 1 S.C.R. 863, at p. 965. This broad discretion should not be narrowed by "casting it in a straight-jacket of judicially prescribed conditions": Ward, at para. 18. But this does not mean that Charter breaches should always, or even routinely, be remedied by awards of Charter damages. The remedy of damages is limited to situations in which it is "appropriate and just" because it serves one or more of the compensatory, vindicatory and deterrent purposes which support that choice of remedy: Ward, at para. 32. Countervailing factors may establish that damages are not an appropriate and just remedy even though they would serve these ends: Ward, at para. 33.
[28] The list of countervailing factors is not closed. So far, two have been identified: the existence of alternative remedies and concerns for good governance: Ward, at para. 33; see also para. 42. I conclude, therefore, that Ward does not preclude the immunity of the Board to Charter damages. Rather, Ward set out two countervailing factors that could negate the appropriateness of Charter damages and specifically left open the development of others.
[29] The jurisprudence does not require that every pleaded claim for Charter damages be assessed on an individualized, case-by-case basis. Ward, for example, specifically contemplates the development of new defences to Charter damages claims and these defences are not limited to enhanced liability thresholds. Countervailing factors against granting Charter damages may be of a more generalized nature, reflecting the availability of other remedies, the accumulated wisdom of the common law and strong indications of public policy.
[emphasis added]
[119] In Ernst, the existence of judicial review as a more appropriate remedy to address the essence of the plaintiff's complaints weighed heavily against the continued survival of a claim based in Charter damages:
[30] First, there is an alternative remedy -- judicial review -- that substantially addresses the alleged Charter breach. Judicial review is available to vindicate Charter rights and to clarify the law so as to prevent similar future breaches. Second, good governance concerns are also engaged as granting damages undermines the effectiveness of the Board and inhibits effective governance. Third, to determine the appropriateness of Charter damages against this type of board on a case-by-case basis in a highly factual and contextual manner largely undermines the purposes served by an immunity.
[31] When these countervailing factors are considered collectively -- that is, when one looks at their cumulative effect -- they negate the appropriateness of an otherwise functionally justified award of Charter damages against this Board. In short, damages are not an appropriate and just remedy for the Board's Charter breaches.
[120] Ernst placed considerable weight on the greater efficacy of judicial review for addressing the plaintiff's complaints:
[34] The availability of judicial review is important for two reasons.
[35] First, judicial review can provide substantial and effective relief against alleged Charter breaches by a quasi-judicial and regulatory board like this one. The facts of this case strikingly illustrate the utility of the remedy of judicial review. The basis of Ms. Ernst's complaint is that the Board abused its discretion and breached the Charter by refusing to deal with her. If that claim were established in the context of judicial review, a superior court could set aside the directive which Ms. Ernst alleges was issued to stop interaction with her and could order corrective action. Such orders would go a long way towards vindicating Ms. Ernst's Charter rights.
[36] Moreover, judicial review would in all likelihood provide vindication in a much more timely manner than an action for damages. Again, the facts of this case provide a good example of how this could be so. Ms. Ernst did not start her action for damages until some two years after the alleged breach, and several months after the Board had rescinded the directive which she challenged. A prompt application for judicial review had the potential to achieve practical relief much sooner. While an application for judicial review would not have led to an award of damages, it might well have addressed the breach much sooner and thereby significantly reduced the extent of its impact as well as vindicated Ms. Ernst's Charter right to freedom of expression. Finally, judicial review would have provided a convenient process to clarify what the Charter required of the Board. That sort of clarification plays an important role in preventing similar future rights infringements.
[37] Thus, judicial review of the Board's decisions and directives has the potential to provide prompt vindication of Charter rights, to provide effective relief in relation to the Board's conduct in the future, to reduce the extent of any damage flowing from the breach, and to provide legal clarity to help prevent any future breach of a similar nature. While the remedies available under judicial review do not include Charter damages, Ward directs us to consider the existence of alternative remedies, not identical ones: para. 33.
[emphasis added]
[121] Alternative remedies weighing against the appropriateness of Charter damages are not limited to judicial review, the focus of Ernst. In Quinn, the Court struck the claim as bound to fail, based on the existence of other adequate alternative avenues more appropriate than Charter damages:
[62] In my opinion, the existence of alternative remedies both in private law and through the operation of the CFCSA are both compelling countervailing factors that demonstrate the Respondents' claim for Charter damages has no reasonable prospect of success and should not consume scarce judicial resources by going to trial. As Moldaver J. identified in Henry:
[38] Where the state can show that another remedy is available to effectively address a Charter breach -- whether under the Charter or in private law -- a damages claim may be defeated at the third step of Ward. For instance, if a declaration of a Charter breach would adequately achieve the objectives that would otherwise be served by a damages award, then granting damages as well as a declaration would be superfluous, and therefore inappropriate and unjust in the circumstances [citations omitted].
[122] In Quinn, one of the alternative remedies more appropriate than damages for the vindication of the plaintiffs' Charter objections to the removal of their children was "engaging with the processes available" under the statutory scheme (the Child, Family and Community Service Act, RSBC 1996, c 46): paras. 62, 69, 72. Similarly, the plaintiff in the present case could have and ought to have engaged the statutory and other processes available to her to address her Charter concerns: providing her views about the decisions to the College or PH Officer, as the statutorily-designated decision-makers; seeking review of any future hypothetical decisions or investigations by the College of the PH Officer under the provisions of the HP Act or PH Act; and seeking judicial review of those decisions if the outcome was not satisfactory.
[123] The Ernst observations dovetail with those discussed above with respect to the abuse of process posed by the plaintiff's claim. The plaintiff neither pleads nor argues any compelling basis for establishing that Charter damages are "appropriate and just", to address Ward, or that judicial review is not a more appropriate means to address her complaints, to address Ernst. Advancing the claims as Charter damages claims does not exempt those claims from a consideration of the appropriateness of proceeding by way of judicial review. Rather, the formulation of the plaintiff's grievances as Charter damages claims further emphasises and illustrates why these claims are nothing but thinly-veiled, artfully-pleaded attacks on administrative decisions that must be pursued by way of judicial review. She in essence seeks damages on the basis that she disagrees with the manner in which the defendants are conducting their statutory decision-making authority: the quintessential focus of judicial review.
D. Other deficiencies of the claim
[124] Given the conclusions above, these reasons will only touch upon other significant deficiencies in the present and proposed claim.
[125] The 30-page, 80-paragraph PANCC is prolix. Its corpulent paragraphs provide more in the nature of impermissible evidence and argument, than a concise pleading confined to material facts and law. In Mercantile Office Systems Private Limited v. Worldwide Warranty Life Services Inc., 2021 BCCA 362 at paras. 44-52, such violations prompted the Court to strike the impugned pleadings. The PANCC is more a polemic than a pleading. A few examples will provide a flavour:
22. By implication from the general to the particular, the PHO's order made March 7, 2022, declares the Plaintiff to be a health hazard under the Public Health Act. The Plaintiff has natural immunity derived from previous and recent infection. She does not present a hazard to anyone. The PHO's declaration that that Plaintiff is a public health hazard is made in bad faith and in knowing defiance of the well-developed science concluding that vaccinated and unvaccinated people are at equal risk of Omicron infection and infecting others, and in knowing defiance of the well- developed science concluding that persons who recently recovered from Omicron present no appreciable danger to others. The declaration that the Plaintiff is herself a health hazard is offensive and amounts to an old-fashioned abuse of power.
25. Deprivation of the Plaintiff's entitlement to practice her profession is accretive with other deprivations imposed on non-vaccinated persons by orders of the PHO, which include the Plaintiff's exclusion from public gatherings, restaurants, bars, gyms, community centres, sporting and cultural events, and is further accretive of deprivations imposed on non-vaccinated persons by order of the federal government, including restrictions on travel by air and train. The PHO and College are aware of these additional hardships imposed on non-vaccinated persons and, indeed, is counting on them to turn this additional hardship of denying the entitlement to practice naturopathic medicine into a tipping point to compel the Plaintiff, by force of the accretive hardship, to submit to medical treatment to which she does not consent.
.
Toxic Synergy of Infringements
30. The Expressive Restrictions prevent the Plaintiff and like-minded colleagues from engaging in political advocacy, protest activity and other forms of democratic association and expression to prevent the PHO and the Minister from forcing her to submit to unwanted medical treatment.
31. Not only is the Plaintiff forced by the Defendants to submit to unwanted medical treatment, she is restricted from public expression of the weak and unjustified rationale for forced treatment, and restricted from participating in public discourse or engaging in public advocacy against forced treatment. The infringement of free expression synergizes with the infringement of medical autonomy to create a denial of Charter rights that is more toxic than the sum of its parts.
.
34. The purpose of restrictions on expressive freedom is ultimately to promote widespread adoption of and/or submission to mRNA therapy. The concern is that a public exposed to the Natural Immunity Beliefs and/or the Critical Beliefs would be less likely to take or accept mRNA therapy. A further concern is that public debate and critical thought about the safety, efficacy and necessity of mRNA therapy could reduce adoption of mRNA therapy, possibly from misinformation but also as a result of informed lack of consent to treatment. A further concern is that public discussion about the Natural Immunity Beliefs and/or the Critical Beliefs could foment distrust and skepticism about the present and future decisions of the PHO and the Minister, given their clear errors in judgment and the dramatic failure to achieve herd immunity. Aspects of these objectives cannot be recognized as pressing and substantial objectives consistent with a free and democratic society as they are inherently inimical in their very purpose to the rights guaranteed under the Charter.
48. These federal and provincial prohibitions, just like the Defendants' threats to compel forced treatment on medical practitioners, were intended at least in part to create state-imposed psychological stress on unvaccinated people to encourage them to submit to unwanted vaccination treatment. The stress was imposed by means of prohibition from participation in public life, a degree of isolation from group contact and denial of access to public services and facilities and by public vilification and condemnation and cultivation of animosity and channeling of existing public fear towards hatred and loathing of the unvaccinated by the public at large. The additional threats to terminate the Plaintiff's entitlement to practice her profession is specifically intended to add to the already existing stresses imposed by these other government prohibitions and government vilification and condemnation.
[bold italics added for emphasis; underlining in original to denote proposed amendment]
[126] At the same time, certain allegations in the NCC and PANCC are vague and unparticularised. For example, the claim for special damages is unparticularised, although particulars are required for such a demand: Bakhtiari v. British Columbia (Minister of Justice), 2017 BCSC 708 at para. 24; Driscol v. MacBain, 2002 SKQB 244 at para. 12.
[127] As another example of a pleading deficiency, the plaintiff alleges that "[t]he PHO and the Minister, or employees of the Province acting under their direction or supervision, encouraged, directed, pressured and ordered the College to issue the March 17, 2020 Threat to registrants of the College", without providing particulars of those actions. And those alleged actions are apparently not material facts but gratuitous verbiage: the plaintiff makes no actual claim against the provincial defendants with respect to those allegations.
[128] The claim also purports to advance claims based on doctrines that do not in themselves constitute free-standing causes of action. The NCC and PANCC base the relief sought with respect to the PH Act solely upon the "rule of law": paras 1:11-14; 2:1(c). The rule of law is an unwritten constitutional principle; it cannot be used as a freestanding principle to invalidate legislation: British Columbia v. Imperial Tobacco Canada Ltd., 2005 SCC 49 at paras. 57-59.
[129] Finally, the NCC and PANCC misname "His Majesty the King in Right of the Province of British Columbia" as the defendant. To the extent the plaintiff is seeking a declaration that aspects of the PH Act and orders made by the PH Officer are unconstitutional, the proper defendant is the Attorney General of British Columbia: Allen v. Superintendent of Motor Vehicles and Attorney General of British Columbia (1986), 2 BCLR (2d) 255 (SC) at 260-61, per Madam Justice Southin; Maddock at para. 5, per Chief Justice Hinkson; Klassen v. British Columbia (Attorney General), 2021 BCSC 2254 at para. 11, per Mr Justice Jenkins; the last two cases were in the context of the pandemic. Further, "His Majesty the King in right of the Provincial Health Officer" is not a legal entity. The PH Officer is an independent office appointed by Order in Council and ought to be named separately as a defendant: PH Act, ss. 64-69. As stated in Karen Horsman & Gareth Morley, Government Liability: Law and Practice (Toronto: Thomson Reuters) (loose-leaf updated 2023, release 3), at 2:2:[9]
In order to enter into contracts, own property, sue or be sued, an entity must have "civil personality". Individuals and business corporations have civil personality, as does the Crown. Public sector organizations may have their own civil personality, they may be part of the Crown or they may only have public law existence. While sometimes it will be clear from the statute that creates the organization which category it falls into, sometimes it is a difficult question.
A governmental entity can "exist" legally for either public-law or private-law purposes. If a statute gives an individual or group a power of decision, then a person affected by the decision may seek an order in the nature of certiorari, prohibition or mandamus if the body has acted outside its jurisdiction, threatens to act outside its jurisdiction or refuses to perform a mandatory statutory duty. The statute may also give persons affected a right of appeal, either to another body or to a court. Finally, a person may seek a Charter remedy against the body if it is part of "government" as that term is defined in s. 32 of the Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms.
However, the legal existence of an entity for administrative and constitutional purposes must be kept strictly distinct from whether it exists for civil purposes. For example, if a Minister makes a statutory decision, and an affected person wishes to challenge it for administrative or constitutional reasons, the Minister is the proper respondent to a petition or application for judicial review. However, all of the executive acts of the Minister will be attributed to the Crown, all of his or her department's employees will be employees of the Crown (and not the Minister) and the appropriate institutional defendant in a civil action against decisions of the Ministry will be the Crown. On the other hand, if a governmental organization does have its own legal personality (unlike a Ministry or Department), then that organization, and not the Crown, is the appropriate defendant in a civil action. Failure to appreciate this can lead to the action being struck out.
[130] The Court accordingly grants the order sought by the provincial defendants: that the Attorney General of British Columbia and Dr Bonnie Henry in her capacity as Provincial Health Officer for the Province of British Columbia be substituted in place of His Majesty the King in Right of the Province of British Columbia as defendants in this proceeding, and the style of cause be amended accordingly.
E. Remedy
[131] Greengen sets out the remedies where an action is found to be a collateral attack on an administrative decision:
[64] Where an action is a collateral attack on an administrative decision, the action is stayed generally (as contemplated in TeleZone), dismissed (as in Stewart) or struck (as in Gulf Coast). All of these remedies are contemplated in Rule 9-5(1) of the Supreme Court Civil Rules.
[132] In their responses to civil claim, both sets of defendants placed the plaintiff on notice of the myriad material deficiencies in her claim surveyed above. As noted, the plaintiff did not heed these admonitions, but rather doubled down on proceeding with an action that is, in essence, an application for judicial review properly brought by petition. The plaintiff also persisted in advancing a claim for a hypothetical wrong that had not materialised, and indeed has never materialised. As noted in the introduction, in contrast to many strike applications, we have the benefit of the plaintiff's PANCC providing an advance review of an amended claim. With her claim facing a strike application, the plaintiff has presumably put her best foot forward.
[133] A claim should not be struck where, if amended, it could disclose a reasonable cause of action. But if the claim is so defective that it is incapable of amendment, it may be struck in its entirety, without leave to amend: Olumide v. British Columbia (Human Rights Tribunal), 2019 BCCA 386 at para. 10. As stated in The Owners, Strata Plan LMS3259 v. Sze Hang Holding Inc., 2009 BCSC 473, rev'd on other grounds 2012 BCCA 196:
[40] Generally, if the problem with a pleading is that it is inadequately drafted, a party will be given the opportunity to redraft it. However, if it is plain and obvious that even if redrafted a pleading is bound to fail because it does not raise an arguable issue (that is, it is without legal foundation), a party will not be granted the opportunity to redraft: Braun Investment Group Inc. v. Emco Investment Corp. (1984), 58 B.C.L.R. 396, 46 C.P.C. 85 (S.C.), aff'd (1985), 67 B.C.L.R. 247 (C.A.); and McNaughton v. Baker (1988), 25 B.C.L.R. (2d) 17, [1988] 4 W.W.R. 742 (C.A.).
[45] Some of the proposed defences and claims of the Defendants could not be redrafted in any event as they are without legal foundation. That is, no matter how they are redrafted, they are bound to fail because they do not raise an arguable issue.
[134] While certain defects surveyed above (e.g., the lack of particulars) could in theory be remedied through amendment, the essential elements of the claim--its collateral attack on administrative decisions, and the inappropriateness of Charter damages--cannot. The plaintiff does not in the alternative seek leave to provide an alternative proposed notice of civil claim, and has provided no basis to conclude that the claim is salvageable: Kindylides at para. 51.
[135] Given the manifold difficulties identified above, and the fact that the plaintiff has already provided the PANCC which suffers from the same defects, the claim is irredeemable through amendment. Following the disposition in Quinn, Ernst, and Stewart, the claim is struck in its entirety, and dismissed, without leave to amend.
IV. CONCLUSION
[136] The defendants have been successful in their applications, and in this proceeding. They are presumptively entitled to their costs of the application and action, at scale B. If any party wishes to dislodge this presumption, that party will advise the others within 15 days of these reasons, and schedule with the Registry a date as soon as reasonably practicable to argue the matter. Each party will provide a written argument to the other parties and to the Court at least seven days before the hearing date.
[137] The Court thanks counsel for their comprehensive and compelling submissions. Although these reasons have been critical of the plaintiff's pleadings, they should not be read as a criticism of her counsel, who advanced zealous and creative advocacy of behalf of his client.
